natural_occurrence.description: A colorless, highly flammable gas commonly used as fuel and as a propellant in food aerosol products. Liquefied under moderate pressure for storage and transport.

manufacturing.method: petroleum refining and natural gas processing
manufacturing.description: Extracted from natural gas or produced as a byproduct of petroleum refining and natural gas processing. Separated from other hydrocarbons through fractional distillation and purified for various uses including food-grade applications.

other_uses.cosmetics: Aerosol propellant in hairsprays and other products
other_uses.industrial: Fuel (BBQ grills, heating), refrigerant, chemical feedstock, vehicle fuel
other_uses.household: BBQ fuel, portable heaters, camping equipment, forklift fuel

eu.conditions: Authorized as propellant and extraction solvent

usa.notes: Approved as propellant and extraction solvent

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Carrier or Extraction Solvents
canada.notes: Approved as propellant and extraction solvent

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (acceptable for use as propellant)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (no toxicological concern when used as propellant)

commonly_found_in: BBQ grills and outdoor cooking (main use)

adi_context.source_note: No ADI needed - propane evaporates immediately from food products and is not consumed in significant amounts.

fun_facts: The same gas in your cooking spray and your BBQ grill
Most common fuel for outdoor grilling and camping
Also known as LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) when mixed with butane
Highly flammable - never spray near open flames!
Evaporates instantly when sprayed - doesn't remain in food
Often mixed with butane and isobutane in aerosol cans
